# memphisbankruptcy.org -- Full Content

This file contains the full text content of all pages on memphisbankruptcy.org.
Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project (openbankruptcyproject.org).


---

## Memphis Bankruptcy -- Chapter 7 & 13 Filing Guide [2026]
Source: https://memphisbankruptcy.org/

Memphis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Memphis Bankruptcy -- Your Complete Filing Guide for 2026

You are not alone. Over 173,000 cases have been filed in the Western District of Tennessee since 2008. This free guide will help you understand your options, know what to expect, and make informed decisions.

79.6% of Memphis Chapter 13 cases are dismissed -- among the highest rates nationally

Read the FAQ
Check Your Eligibility

173,823

W.D. Tenn. Cases (2008-2024)

79.6%

Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate

53.5%

Prior Filer Rate

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13: Which Is Right for You?

Chapter 7 -- Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test based on your income.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical debt

Must pass the means test

May lose non-exempt property

Filing fee: $338

Learn more about Chapter 7 →

Chapter 13 -- Payment Plan

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Good for saving a home from foreclosure or a car from repossession. No means test required.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

Catch up on missed payments

No means test requirement

Filing fee: $313

Learn more about Chapter 13 →

Important note for Memphis filers: The W.D. Tenn. has a 79.6% Chapter 13 dismissal rate -- nearly 4 out of 5 cases end without a discharge. More than half of filers (53.5%) have filed before. If you are considering Chapter 13, understand the risks and ensure you have strong representation. See full statistics.

Not sure which chapter fits? Compare them in detail.

Memphis Chapter 13: Key Statistics

Memphis has some of the highest Chapter 13 dismissal rates nationally:

79.6% dismissal rate -- nearly 4 out of 5 Chapter 13 cases end without a discharge

Only 20.4% of filers receive a discharge -- meaning only 1 in 5 successfully complete their plan

53.5% are prior filers -- more than half have filed at least once before

Only 1.4% file pro se -- nearly all filers have attorney representation, yet the dismissal rate remains high

An estimated 4,861 cases may involve potential 1328(f) eligibility violations

These numbers indicate that a significant number of Memphis residents file Chapter 13 cases that do not reach completion, often after making payments for months or years.

View full statistics →

What to Expect When Filing

The Process at a Glance

Credit counseling -- You must complete a credit counseling course within 180 days before filing. Cost: about $15-25.

File your petition -- Your attorney files the petition, schedules, and statements with the court. The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and foreclosures.

341 meeting -- About 30 days after filing, you attend a brief meeting of creditors. The trustee asks questions under oath. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Debtor education course -- You must complete a second financial education course before discharge. Cost: about $10-25.

Discharge -- Chapter 7: about 60 days after the 341 meeting. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Detailed timeline and what to expect →

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost?

The total cost depends on the chapter you file and your specific situation:

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (can request installment payments)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$1,800 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13 in Memphis

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines.

Full cost breakdown → | National cost guide

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Chapter 13 dismissal rate in Memphis?

The W.D. Tenn. has a 79.6% Chapter 13 dismissal rate -- one of the highest in the nation. Only about 1 in 5 filers successfully complete their plan. Full statistics.

How much does bankruptcy cost in Memphis?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees typically range from $1,000-$1,800 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13. Full cost breakdown.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Tennessee?

Tennessee's homestead exemption is $5,000 (individual) or $7,500 (family). This is one of the lowest in the country. If your equity exceeds the exemption, your home could be at risk in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home. See all exemptions.

Can I keep my car if I file bankruptcy?

Tennessee's vehicle exemption was increased to $10,000 in 2024. If your car equity is below that limit, you can keep it in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car and catch up on payments. Learn about exemptions.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year plan. The 341 meeting happens about 30 days after filing. Full timeline.

See all frequently asked questions →

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Need Free Bankruptcy Help?

If you cannot afford a bankruptcy attorney, free help is available. Legal aid organizations, law school clinics, and court self-help programs can assist you at no cost.

Find Free Help in Tennessee

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Memphis -- Eligibility, Exemptions, Timeline
Source: https://memphisbankruptcy.org/chapter-7.html

Memphis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Memphis

Chapter 7 is the fastest path to debt relief. Most unsecured debts are eliminated in 3-4 months. Here is what Memphis residents need to know.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7, sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy" or a "fresh start," eliminates most unsecured debts. A court-appointed trustee reviews your assets. If you have non-exempt property, the trustee may sell it to pay creditors -- but most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where the filer keeps everything.

In Memphis, most Chapter 7 filers keep all their property because it falls within Tennessee exemption limits.

What Debts Does Chapter 7 Eliminate?

Dischargeable (eliminated)

Credit card debt

Medical bills

Personal loans and payday loans

Old utility bills

Deficiency balances from repossessions

Some older tax debts

Civil judgments (in most cases)

Not dischargeable

Student loans (except in rare hardship cases)

Recent income taxes (generally last 3 years)

Child support and alimony

Court-ordered restitution and fines

Debts from fraud or willful injury

Certain debts under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)

The Means Test

To file Chapter 7, you must pass the means test. This compares your household income to the state median:

Below median income: You automatically qualify for Chapter 7

Above median income: A detailed calculation of your disposable income determines eligibility

For a single filer in Tennessee, the current median is approximately $52,000/year. These figures increase with household size.

Tennessee Exemptions in Chapter 7

Tennessee exemptions are among the lowest in the nation. The homestead exemption of $5,000 provides minimal protection for homeowners.

PropertyTennessee Exemption

Homestead$5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (family)

Vehicle$10,000 (increased 2024)

Personal property$10,000

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Public benefitsFully exempt

Full exemption details →

Chapter 7 Timeline

Weeks 1-2: Preparation -- Gather documents (tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements). Complete credit counseling course ($15-25).

Day 1: Filing -- Petition filed with the court. Automatic stay takes effect immediately.

Day 30-45: 341 Meeting -- Brief meeting of creditors. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Day 30-60: Debtor education -- Complete the second required financial education course ($10-25).

Day 90-120: Discharge -- The court enters your discharge order, eliminating eligible debts.

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 discharges most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not discharge student loans, recent taxes, child support, or debts from fraud. See section 523(a) for the full list.

What is the means test?

The means test compares your household income to the Tennessee median. If below, you qualify automatically. If above, a detailed calculation determines eligibility.

How long does Chapter 7 take in Memphis?

From filing to discharge, typically 3-4 months. The 341 meeting occurs about 30 days after filing, and the discharge comes about 60 days after that.

What property can I keep in Chapter 7?

Tennessee exemptions protect homestead equity up to $5,000/$7,500, vehicle up to $10,000, and personal property up to $10,000. Full exemption details.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Memphis -- Payment Plans, Dismissal Rates, Timeline
Source: https://memphisbankruptcy.org/chapter-13.html

Memphis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Memphis

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. But in Memphis, the dismissal rate is alarming -- meaning many filers never complete their plan.

79.6% of Memphis Chapter 13 cases are dismissed -- among the worst in the nation

79.6%

W.D. Tenn. Dismissal Rate

53.5%

Prior Filer Rate

3-5 yrs

Plan Duration

$313

Filing Fee

What Is Chapter 13?

Chapter 13, sometimes called a "wage earner's plan," allows people with regular income to create a court-supervised repayment plan lasting 3 to 5 years. You make monthly payments to a bankruptcy trustee, who distributes the money to your creditors.

Unlike Chapter 7, Chapter 13 does not require liquidating assets. You keep all your property. At the end of the plan, remaining eligible debts are discharged.

Chapter 13 is particularly useful for:

Saving a home from foreclosure by catching up on missed mortgage payments

Keeping a car while catching up on auto loan payments

Paying tax debts or other priority debts over time

Protecting non-exempt property that would be lost in Chapter 7

Filers who do not qualify for Chapter 7 under the means test

Memphis Dismissal Rate

The data tells a concerning story. A dismissed Chapter 13 means the debtor spent months or years making payments but received no discharge -- no debt relief.

Western District of Tennessee (W.D. Tenn.)

79.6% dismissed

Out of 173,823 cases filed between 2008 and 2024, only 20.4% achieved a successful discharge. The prior filer rate is 53.5% -- meaning roughly 1 in 3 filers have been through the system before.

Why it matters: A dismissed Chapter 13 means the automatic stay ends, creditors resume collection, and the debtor receives no discharge. Months or years of payments produced no lasting relief. Strong legal representation is critical. View full statistics.

How a Chapter 13 Plan Works

The Monthly Payment

Your monthly payment is calculated based on your disposable income. The payment must cover:

Priority debts in full: Recent taxes, child support arrears, attorney fees

Secured debt arrears: Mortgage arrears, car loan arrears

Trustee fees: Typically around 10% of plan payments

Unsecured creditors: Whatever remains goes to credit cards, medical bills, etc.

What Happens at the End?

After completing all plan payments, the court enters a discharge under 11 U.S.C. section 1328. If you received a prior discharge, time bars under section 1328(f) may prevent another discharge.

Chapter 13 Timeline

Before filing: Complete credit counseling ($15-25). Gather income documentation.

Day 1 -- Filing: Petition filed. Automatic stay takes effect.

Day 30: First plan payment due to the trustee.

Day 30-45: 341 meeting of creditors.

Day 45-120: Confirmation hearing.

Years 1-5: Monthly payments to the trustee.

Plan completion: Complete debtor education course. Court enters discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Chapter 13 dismissal rate in Memphis?

The Western District of Tennessee has a 79.6% Chapter 13 dismissal rate based on 173,823 cases (2008-2024). The prior filer rate is 53.5%.

How long is a Chapter 13 plan?

Plans last 3 to 5 years. Below-median income may qualify for 3 years; above-median requires 5 years.

Can I keep my house in Chapter 13?

Yes. The automatic stay stops foreclosure immediately and you can catch up on missed payments through your plan.

What happens if my Chapter 13 is dismissed?

The automatic stay ends, creditors resume collection, and you receive no discharge. You may be able to file again, but there may be waiting periods.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay

## Tennessee Bankruptcy Exemptions -- What You Can Keep -- Memphis
Source: https://memphisbankruptcy.org/exemptions.html

Memphis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Tennessee Bankruptcy Exemptions

Exemptions determine what property you get to keep when you file bankruptcy. Understanding them is critical -- they can mean the difference between keeping and losing your home, car, and savings.

What Are Exemptions?

Bankruptcy exemptions are laws that protect certain property from being taken to pay your creditors. In Chapter 7, non-exempt property can be sold by the trustee. In Chapter 13, the value of non-exempt property affects how much you must pay through your plan.

Tennessee exemptions are among the lowest in the nation. The homestead exemption of $5,000 provides minimal protection for homeowners.

Tennessee Exemptions -- Full List

Homestead -- $5,000/$7,500

Protects up to $5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (family) of equity in your primary residence. Equity is your home's value minus what you owe on the mortgage.

Tenn. Code Ann. 26-2-301

Motor Vehicle -- $10,000

Protects up to $10,000 (increased 2024) of equity in a motor vehicle.

Tenn. Code Ann. 26-2-103

Personal Property -- $10,000

$10,000 personal property aggregate

Other Tennessee Exemptions

PropertyAmount

Retirement accounts (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Social Security benefitsFully exempt

Unemployment benefitsFully exempt

Workers' compensationFully exempt

Health aidsFully exempt

Public assistance benefitsFully exempt

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Tennessee homestead exemption?

Tennessee's homestead exemption protects $5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (family) of equity in your primary residence.

How much vehicle equity can I protect?

Tennessee's vehicle exemption is $10,000 (increased 2024). You can supplement this with other available exemptions.

Are retirement accounts protected?

Yes. ERISA-qualified retirement accounts (401k, pension) are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. IRAs are protected up to federal limits.

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Memphis? -- Filing Fees, Attorney Fees
Source: https://memphisbankruptcy.org/cost.html

Memphis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Memphis?

Bankruptcy has real costs, but they are often far less than the debt you are trying to eliminate. Here is a transparent breakdown of every cost you should expect.

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$25-50

Credit Counseling (2 courses)

$1,000-$4,000

Typical Attorney Fees

Chapter 7 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$338Can pay in installments; fee waiver available

Attorney fees$1,000-$1,800Typical Memphis range; usually paid upfront

Pre-filing credit counseling$15-$25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10-$25Required before discharge

Fee Waiver (Chapter 7 Only)

If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines, you can apply to have the $338 filing fee waived entirely. You file the fee waiver application (Official Form 103B) with your petition.

Chapter 13 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$313Can be paid through your plan

Attorney fees$2,500-$4,000Typical Memphis range; mostly paid through plan

Pre-filing credit counseling$15-$25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10-$25Required before discharge

The Chapter 13 Payment Advantage

Attorney fees and the filing fee can be included in your repayment plan. You typically need only a small upfront payment (often $0-$500), with the rest paid through monthly plan payments over 3-5 years.

Ways to Reduce Costs

Free and Low-Cost Options

Memphis Area Legal Services -- provides free bankruptcy representation for qualifying low-income individuals

Fee waiver -- eliminates the $338 Chapter 7 filing fee for income below 150% of poverty

Installment payments -- pay the filing fee in up to 4 installments over 120 days

Pro se filing -- filing without an attorney is possible but not recommended (only 1.4% of W.D. Tenn. filers do this)

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Memphis?

Filing fee: $338. Attorney fees: $1,000-$1,800. Credit counseling: $25-50 total. Fee waivers available for low-income filers. National cost comparison.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Memphis?

Filing fee: $313. Attorney fees: $2,500-$4,000. Most costs can be paid through your repayment plan.

Can I file bankruptcy for free?

The filing fee can be waived for low-income Chapter 7 filers. Memphis Area Legal Services may provide free representation.

Do I have to pay an attorney upfront?

For Chapter 7, usually yes. For Chapter 13, fees are typically included in your plan.

## What to Expect When Filing Bankruptcy in Memphis -- Timeline, 341 Meeting, Discharge
Source: https://memphisbankruptcy.org/what-to-expect.html

Memphis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

What to Expect When Filing Bankruptcy

The bankruptcy process can feel overwhelming, but it follows a clear, predictable path. Here is exactly what happens from start to finish in Memphis.

Chapter 7 Timeline

1-4 weeks before filing

Preparation

Complete a credit counseling course ($15-25). Gather documents: tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements, and a list of all debts and assets.

Day 1

Filing Day

Your petition is filed with the Western District of Tennessee. The automatic stay takes effect immediately. All collection activity must stop.

Day 21-40

341 Meeting of Creditors

Brief meeting of creditors at the courthouse (200 Jefferson Ave, Suite 413, Memphis, TN 38103). The trustee verifies your identity and asks questions. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Day 21-60

Debtor Education Course

Complete a second financial education course ($10-25). File the certificate with the court.

Day 60-90 (after 341)

Discharge

The court enters your discharge order. Eligible debts are eliminated. Total time: typically 3-4 months.

Chapter 13 Timeline

Before filing

Preparation

Complete credit counseling. Work with your attorney to develop a feasible repayment plan.

Day 1

Filing Day

Petition and proposed plan filed. Automatic stay takes effect. Foreclosures and repossessions stop.

Day 30

First Plan Payment

First monthly payment due to the trustee, even if your plan has not been confirmed yet.

Day 45-120

Confirmation Hearing

The court decides whether to approve your plan.

Years 1-5

Plan Payments

Make monthly payments. Report income changes. File tax returns on time.

After plan completion

Discharge

Complete debtor education. Court enters a section 1328 discharge.

Important: In the Western District of Tennessee, 79.6% of Chapter 13 cases are dismissed before plan completion. A dismissed case means no discharge. Strong representation is critical.

The Automatic Stay -- Your Immediate Protection

The automatic stay is one of the most powerful protections in bankruptcy law. The moment your petition is filed, creditors must immediately stop:

Phone calls, letters, and emails demanding payment

Lawsuits and court proceedings against you

Wage garnishments

Bank account levies

Foreclosure proceedings

Vehicle repossession

Utility disconnections (for 20 days)

Creditors who violate the automatic stay can be held in contempt and ordered to pay damages.

Exception: If you have had a prior case dismissed within the past year, the stay may be limited to 30 days or may not apply at all. Check your eligibility before filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens at the 341 meeting?

The 341 meeting is a brief hearing where the trustee asks questions under oath. Bring photo ID and Social Security proof. Most last 5-10 minutes.

What is the automatic stay?

The automatic stay stops all collection activity the moment you file. Violations can result in sanctions.

How long until I get my discharge?

Chapter 7: 3-4 months. Chapter 13: after completing your 3-5 year plan.

Will bankruptcy affect my credit score?

Chapter 7 stays on your report for 10 years; Chapter 13 for 7 years. Many filers see recovery within 1-2 years. Rebuilding after bankruptcy.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay

## Memphis Bankruptcy FAQ -- Common Questions Answered
Source: https://memphisbankruptcy.org/faq.html

Memphis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Frequently Asked Questions

Answers to the most common questions from Memphis residents considering bankruptcy.

Cost and Eligibility

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Memphis?

Chapter 7 filing fee is $338; Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees: $1,000-$1,800 for Chapter 7, $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13. Credit counseling: $25-50 total. Full cost breakdown.

What is the means test?

The means test compares your income to the Tennessee median. Below median = automatic Chapter 7 qualification. Above = detailed calculation.

Can I file bankruptcy more than once?

Yes, with time limits. Use the 1328(f) screener to check. Learn about filing again.

Property and Assets

Will I lose my house?

Your homestead exemption protects $5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (family) of equity. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home and catch up on payments. See all exemptions.

Can I keep my car?

Tennessee protects $10,000 (increased 2024) in vehicle equity. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car. Exemption details.

What happens to my tax refund?

In Chapter 7, your refund may be an asset the trustee can claim if it exceeds exemptions. In Chapter 13, it may need to be turned over. Timing matters.

The Process

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7: 3-4 months. Chapter 13: 3-5 years. Full timeline.

What is the 341 meeting?

A brief hearing about 30 days after filing. The trustee asks questions under oath. Most last 5-10 minutes. Full guide.

What is the automatic stay?

The automatic stay stops all collection activity immediately when you file -- lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures, repossessions, and calls.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes, immediately upon filing. Learn more.

Types of Debt and Life After

Can I file bankruptcy on medical bills?

Yes. Medical bills are fully dischargeable in both chapters.

Will bankruptcy eliminate student loans?

Generally no, except in rare hardship cases. Learn more.

Will bankruptcy affect my credit score?

Chapter 7: 10 years on report. Chapter 13: 7 years. Many see recovery within 1-2 years. Rebuilding tips.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 is faster; Chapter 13 lets you keep property. Given Memphis's 79.6% Chapter 13 dismissal rate, understanding the risks is essential. Compare side by side.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test
Cost Guide

## Memphis Bankruptcy Statistics -- 173,823 Cases Analyzed
Source: https://memphisbankruptcy.org/statistics.html

Memphis Bankruptcy Guide

Memphis Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from 173,823 cases in the Western District of Tennessee, analyzed from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database (2008-2024).

173,823

Total Cases (2008-2024)

79.6%

Dismissal Rate

20.4%

Discharge Rate

53.5%

Prior Filer Rate

4,861

Est. 1328(f) Violations

1.4%

Pro Se Rate

What These Numbers Mean for You

If you are considering Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Memphis, these statistics should inform your decision:

79.6% of cases are dismissed. That means the majority of filers spent months or years making payments and got no debt relief at the end.

Only 20.4% receive a discharge. This is the percentage of filers who successfully complete their plan.

53.5% are prior filers. This high rate suggests many people cycle through the system without achieving lasting relief.

An estimated 4,861 cases may involve potential 1328(f) eligibility violations -- filings where the debtor could never have received a discharge.

Quality of representation matters. The data shows that representation quality is one of the strongest predictors of plan completion.

District Overview: Western District of Tennessee

MetricW.D. Tenn.

Total cases (2008-2024)173,823

Dismissal rate79.6%

Discharge rate20.4%

Prior filer rate53.5%

Pro se rate1.4%

Est. 1328(f) violations4,861

Key Takeaways

The Prior Filer Problem

53.5% of W.D. Tenn. filers had a prior bankruptcy case. This cycling suggests the first filing failed to provide lasting relief -- and raises questions about whether filers were properly screened under section 1328(f).

4,861 Potential 1328(f) Violations

An estimated 4,861 cases may involve eligibility violations under 11 U.S.C. section 1328(f) -- filings where the debtor had a prior discharge within the statutory time bar and could never have received a new discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many bankruptcy cases are filed in Memphis?

The Western District of Tennessee has had 173,823 cases filed between 2008 and 2024.

What is the Chapter 13 success rate?

The discharge rate is 20.4% and the dismissal rate is 79.6%.

What is the prior filer rate?

53.5% of filers had a previous bankruptcy case, suggesting many cycle through the system without lasting relief.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Memphis Bankruptcy Resources -- Courts, Legal Aid, Credit Counseling
Source: https://memphisbankruptcy.org/resources.html

Memphis Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Memphis Bankruptcy Resources

Courts, legal aid organizations, credit counseling agencies, and free educational tools to help you navigate the bankruptcy process.

Bankruptcy Court

Western District of Tennessee -- Bankruptcy Court

Address: 200 Jefferson Ave, Suite 413, Memphis, TN 38103

Phone: (901) 328-3500

Website: tnwb.uscourts.gov

PACER: ecf.tnwb.uscourts.gov

Legal Aid Organizations

Memphis Area Legal Services

Provides free civil legal services to low-income individuals, including bankruptcy representation.

Phone: (901) 523-8822

Website: malsi.org

West Tennessee Legal Services

Phone: (800) 372-8346

Website: wtls.org

Credit Counseling and Debtor Education

Two courses are required: pre-filing credit counseling and post-filing debtor education. You must use agencies approved by the U.S. Trustee Program.

Approved agencies: justice.gov -- approved agencies

Cost: $15-25 per course (reduced fees available)

Format: Online, phone, or in-person

U.S. Trustee Program

Region 8 (includes W.D. Tenn.): (901) 544-3251

Website: justice.gov/ust

Free Educational Tools

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

1328(f) Discharge Eligibility Screener

Automatic Stay Guide

How to File Bankruptcy

Case Dismissed?

File Again?

Dismissal Rate Data

Frequently Asked Questions

Where is the Memphis bankruptcy court?

200 Jefferson Ave, Suite 413, Memphis, TN 38103. Phone: (901) 328-3500.

Is there free legal help for bankruptcy in Memphis?

Yes. Memphis Area Legal Services ((901) 523-8822) provides free services to qualifying individuals.

Where can I take the required credit counseling?

See the approved list at justice.gov/ust. Most courses are $15-25 online.
